summary: Joseph Stalin (Ioseb Besarionis dze Jughashvili) was the Georgian-born dictator who led the Soviet Union as Vladimir Lenin's successor from the 1920s through the Great Patriotic War (World War II) until his death in 1953. His rule is defined by forced collectivization and the Ukrainian famine known as the Holodomor, the Great Purge (Great Terror) of the late 1930s, victory over Nazi Germany after Operation Barbarossa (including the pivotal Battle of Stalingrad), and a postwar antisemitic campaign built around the fabricated "Doctors' Plot." He was also a prolific target of Soviet-era literature and art, from Osip Mandelstam's fatal "Stalin Epigram" and Anna Akhmatova's Requiem to countless propaganda paintings and statues, and was denounced by his successor Nikita Khrushchev in the 1956 "Secret Speech."

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Stalin is identified in giveaways as the Georgian-born Soviet dictator who succeeded Vladimir Lenin and led the USSR through the 1930s and World War II until his 1953 death.

clues.clue: Georgian-born dictator who led the Soviet Union through the 1930s and World War II
examples: name this dictator of the Soviet Union throughout the 1930s and World War II.
This ethnic Georgian led the Soviet Union during World War II. He purged millions of political enemies during the 1930s.

clues.clue: Succeeded Vladimir Lenin and ruled until his death in 1953
examples: name this leader of the Soviet Union after Vladimir Lenin, who led his nation through World War II.
this Soviet leader who succeeded Lenin and ruled until his 1953 death.

works.name: Early Life & Rise to Power
works.description: Born and raised in Georgia, young Stalin wrote Georgian-language poetry under the pen name "Soselo" and memorized the national epic before turning to revolutionary activity in Tiflis and the oil city of Baku; he rose through the party using aliases like "Koba," allying with (and later purging) Nikolai Bukharin to adopt "Socialism in One Country" over Lenin's New Economic Policy.

clues.clue: As a youth in Georgia, wrote Georgian-language poetry (including "To the Moon") under the pen name "Soselo"
examples: During his youth in this region, a leader wrote poems like "Morning" under the name Soselo.
Identify this author of "To the Moon," one of several poems he wrote under the pseudonym Soselo.

clues.clue: As a youth, admired and memorized parts of Shota Rustaveli's The Knight in the Panther's Skin, Georgia's national epic
examples: As a youth, Stalin admired and memorized parts of Shota Rostaveli's The Knight in the Panther's Skin, the national epic of this country where Stalin was born.

clues.clue: Historians dispute whether he personally participated in or merely watched the Tiflis bank robbery, which he had planned
examples: Historians argue whether he participated in or just stood and watched the Tiflis bank robbery, which he planned.

clues.clue: Agitated as a radical in the oil city of Baku, later saying his "last warm feelings for humanity" died when his wife perished from the city's pollution, before gaining military fame at Tsaritsyn
examples: Baku was a center for radicals like this man who said that his "last warm feelings for humanity" died when his wife perished due to Baku's pollution. This man later gained fame leading military operations in Tsaritsyn.

clues.clue: With Nikolai Bukharin's help, adopted the policy of "Socialism in One Country," prioritizing internal development over spreading world revolution
examples: With the help of Nikolai Bukharin, Stalin adopted this policy which specified that the Soviet Union must focus on internal development before trying to focus on the goal of spreading communism to the rest of the world.

works.name: Collectivization & the Holodomor
works.description: Stalin's forced collectivization of agriculture and dekulakization produced the Holodomor, a catastrophic Ukrainian famine denied by journalists like Walter Duranty; parallel drives collectivized Kazakhstan (killing a quarter of its population) and forcibly "modernized" Central Asia through the anti-veiling Hujum campaign, mass deportations, and the purge of the Uzbek SSR's leadership.

clues.clue: Collectivization and dekulakization policies produced the Holodomor, a devastating Ukrainian famine exacerbated by the "Law of Spikelets"; New York Times reporter Walter Duranty publicly disputed Gareth Jones's account of it
examples: The Holodomor occurred under this leader, who initially closed churches to advance state atheism under his Second Five-Year Plan.
New York Times reporter Walter Duranty openly disputed Gareth Jones's account of a disaster under this leader's regime exacerbated by the Law of Spikelets.

clues.clue: Wrote the article "Dizzy with Success" to criticize "excesses" in the collectivization campaign that had produced the famine
examples: He wrote the article "Dizzy with Success" to criticize "excesses" in a campaign he had initiated that led to the holodomor.
This man used the term "dizzy with success" in some speeches and articles.

clues.clue: Deported kulaks to labor camps, worsening the famine, before reversing course after his country was invaded in Operation Barbarossa
examples: This leader exacerbated famine through the deportation of kulaks to labor camps, but was forced to reverse course after his country was invaded during Operation Barbarossa.

clues.clue: Had members of the census bureau TsUNKhu arrested after a census revealed a population decrease
examples: This leader had members of TsUNKhu arrested after a census revealed a population decrease.

clues.clue: The kolkhoz collective-farm system, built through his Five-Year Plans, contributed to a famine that killed a fourth of the population of Kazakhstan
examples: This leader's policy of creating kolkhozes contributed to a famine that killed a fourth of the population of Kazakhstan.
This man implemented a series of Five Year Plans in his country which resulted in the kolkhoz collective farming system.

clues.clue: In Central Asia, launched the Hujum campaign against the veiling of women under the slogan "To the Attack!," whose participants staged public burnings of paranji robes and sparked the Chust Affair protest
examples: The hujum campaign against the veiling of women occurred under this leader, whose arrest of writers such as Abdurauf Fitrat effectively ended the reformist Jadidi movement.

clues.clue: Forcibly transported hundreds of thousands of ethnic Koreans and Tatars via cattle cars to Muslim-majority Central Asia
examples: This leader's forces used cattle cars to transport hundreds of thousands of ethnic Koreans and Tatars to a Muslim-majority region in one of his forced population transfer programs.

clues.clue: Executed the leaders of the Uzbek Soviet Socialist Republic during his 1930s purges
examples: name this leader who had the leaders of the Uzbek Soviet Socialist Republic executed during his purges in the 1930s.

clues.clue: Closed churches to advance state atheism under his Second Five-Year Plan, before reversing course during the Great Patriotic War
examples: who initially closed churches to advance state atheism under his Second Five-Year Plan prior to reversing course during the Great Patriotic War.

works.name: The Great Purge / Great Terror
works.description: Sparked by the assassination of Sergei Kirov, Stalin's Great Purge of the late 1930s used show trials (the Trial of the Sixteen, the Shakhty and Metropolitan-Vickers "wrecking" trials) and NKVD chiefs Nikolai Yezhov and Lavrentiy Beria to execute or imprison huge numbers of Communist Party members, military officers like Mikhail Tukhachevsky, and former allies like Nikolai Bukharin, while sending dissidents like Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn to the gulag and erasing purged figures from official photographs.

clues.clue: Ordered the Great Purge (Great Terror) of the late 1930s, sparked by the assassination of Sergei Kirov and including the show trials of Kamenev and Zinoviev (the Trial of the Sixteen)
examples: Name this program of the late 1930s that began with the Trial of the Sixteen. Sparked by the assassination of Kirov, the show trials of Kamenev and Zinoviev were part of this program.
Name this Soviet dictator who executed hundreds of thousands in his Great Purge of the 1930s.

clues.clue: Purged his former ally Nikolai Bukharin, with whom he had earlier collaborated on "Socialism in One Country"
examples: Stalin was originally allied with this man, before he had this author of Imperialism and World Economy killed during the Great Purge.
name this author of the pamphlet "Dialectical and Historical Materialism" who purged folks like Nikolai Bukharin.

clues.clue: Was accused by Leon Trotsky of exploiting antisemitism during the Great Purge
examples: Name this man, accused by Leon Trotsky of exploiting antisemitism in his Great Purge of the Soviet government.

clues.clue: Appointed Nikolai Yezhov to head the NKVD ahead of the Purge; Yezhov claimed a "center of centers" master conspiracy existed
examples: Stalin appointed this man as head of the NKVD prior to the Great Purge. This successor of Yagoda claimed that a "center of centers," or a master conspiracy against the USSR, existed.

clues.clue: Later appointed Lavrentiy Beria to lead the secret-police NKVD, following the model of Lenin's Cheka
examples: Stalin appointed Lavrentiy Beria to lead the NKVD, which was this type of organization.
This person led Stalin's NKVD secret police for almost all of World War II.

clues.clue: Executed the military theorist Mikhail Tukhachevsky in a purge of the Red Army's generals, weakening the army before Hitler's invasion
examples: Tukhachevsky blamed the Red Army's failure to capture Warsaw on this future dictator of the Soviet Union, who executed Tukhachevsky during his Great Purge of the Red Army's Generals.
The Red Army was weakened before Hitler's invasion by the purges ordered by this paranoid leader.

clues.clue: The Shakhty Trial and other accusations of "wrecking" (sabotage), later echoed in the Metropolitan-Vickers Trial, constituted part of the Great Purge
examples: During this man's time in office, the Shakhty Trial and other accusations of "wrecking" constituted part of the Great Purge.
Several diplomats tried to obtain assurances from this Soviet leader that foreign citizens working in Russia would be protected in the wake of the Metro-Vickers Trial.

clues.clue: Sent Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn to a gulag for criticizing him
examples: name this leader who sent Alexander Solzhenitsyn to a gulag.
Alexander Solzhenitsyn was sent to a gulag for criticizing this Georgian-born Soviet leader.

clues.clue: Katyusha rocket-launcher designer Georgy Langemak was executed during the Great Purge
examples: This weapon was designed by Georgy Langemak, who was subsequently executed during the Great Purge.

clues.clue: Had lackeys doctor official photographs to erase purged enemies like Lev Kamenev and Grigory Zinoviev
examples: Many images of this man later had political enemies such as Lev Kamenev and Grigory Zinoviev removed.
He was a leader of the Soviet Union known for his purges and having his lackeys doctor photos.

works.name: World War II: Barbarossa & the Eastern Front
works.description: After signing the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact with Nazi Germany to split Poland, Stalin was invaded in Operation Barbarossa; his NKVD carried out the Katyn Forest Massacre of Polish officers, later blamed on the Nazis, while he negotiated as one of the "Big Three" with Churchill and Roosevelt and picked Vyacheslav Molotov to lead the Soviet atomic bomb project.

clues.clue: Signed the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact with Nazi Germany, splitting Poland in half, before Hitler broke it with Operation Barbarossa
examples: He fabricated the Doctors' Plot and was in power when the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact was signed.
Name this country that was split in half by the Soviet Union and Germany in the Molotov-Ribbentrop pact.

clues.clue: The Red Army, weakened by his prewar purges, was invaded by Germany in Operation Barbarossa, which broke the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact
examples: Identify this military campaign that broke the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact.
name this leader who was leader of the USSR when Hitler launched Operation Barbarossa.

clues.clue: His secret police (the NKVD, under Lavrentiy Beria) perpetrated the Katyn Forest Massacre of Polish officers and intelligentsia, later blamed on the Nazis in Soviet textbooks
examples: Soviet textbooks blamed the Nazis for this 1940 massacre of Polish soldiers and intelligentsia, though it was actually perpetrated by the NKVD under Lavrenty Beria.
implementing atrocities like the Katyn Forest Massacre.

clues.clue: Met with Churchill and Roosevelt as one of the "Big Three" at the Yalta and Tehran Conferences
examples: name this Georgian, depicted sitting with Churchill and Roosevelt at the Yalta and Tehran conferences.
Iran hosted the "Big Three" during this conflict.

clues.clue: Operation Bagration is called the "fifth of the ten blows" attributed to him in Soviet accounts of the Great Patriotic War
examples: In works on the Great Patriotic War, Operation Bagration is called the fifth of the ten blows of this Soviet leader who succeeded Lenin and ruled until his 1953 death.

clues.clue: Picked Vyacheslav Molotov to lead the Soviet atomic bomb project
examples: The city of Perm was renamed for this Soviet leader, whom Stalin picked to lead the Soviet project to develop an atomic bomb.

clues.clue: Relied on general Georgy Zhukov, who had defeated the Kwantung Army at Khalkhin Gol, occupied Bessarabia, and organized the defense of Leningrad and later Berlin
examples: Name this decorated general who defeated the Kwantung Army early in his career at the Battle of Khalkhin Gol, and then directed the occupation of Bessarabia.
This Russian general applied the strategies of mechanized warfare he learned at Khalkhin Gol to battles on the Eastern Front of World War II.

works.name: The Battle of Stalingrad
works.description: Fought over the city on the Volga renamed for the Soviet premier, the Battle of Stalingrad turned the tide of the Eastern Front when Georgy Zhukov's Operation Uranus encircled and forced the surrender of Field Marshal Friedrich Paulus's 6th Army, after German forces trapped in "the Cauldron" resorted to eating their own horses.

clues.clue: Turned the tide of the Eastern Front after Georgy Zhukov's Operation Uranus encircled the German 6th Army, ending with the capture of Field Marshal Friedrich Paulus
examples: The tide of battle turned after Georgy Zhukov unleashed Operation Uranus, and this battle ended shortly after the capture of Friedrich Paulus.
Zhukov helped plan Operation Uranus, encircling and forcing the surrender of the Nazi 6th Army, during the long fight over this southern Russian city on the Volga.

clues.clue: Trapped German forces in a pocket they dubbed "the Cauldron," where they were forced to eat their own horses
examples: One side in this battle was trapped in a pocket which they dubbed 'the Cauldron' where they were forced to eat their own horses.

clues.clue: German invaders were stalled by the 1077th anti-aircraft regiment, composed mostly of young women just out of high school, while a local tractor factory was modified to produce T-34 tanks
examples: In this battle, the invaders were stalled by the 1077th anti-aircraft regiment, which was composed mostly of women just out of high school.
During this battle, a tractor factory was modified to produce T-34 tanks.

clues.clue: Erich von Manstein's failed Operation Winter Storm, an attempt to break the encirclement with the German 4th Panzer Army, was followed by the final Soviet assault, Operation Ring
examples: Erich von Manstein oversaw this operation, an attempt by the German 4th Panzer Army to break the Soviet encirclement of the 6th Army. Its failure was followed by the final Soviet assault, Operation Ring.

clues.clue: David Glantz's book Armageddon at Stalingrad debunks several myths about the battle
examples: David Glantz has debunked several myths about this battle in a book titled for 'Armageddon at' it.

works.name: Postwar Anti-Semitic Campaigns
works.description: In his final years, Stalin fabricated the "Doctors' Plot," accusing predominantly Jewish physicians of conspiring to assassinate Soviet leaders, part of a broader campaign against "rootless cosmopolitans" that included ordering the death of theater director Solomon Mikhoels and forcing Jews toward the remote Jewish Autonomous Oblast around Birobidzhan.

clues.clue: Fabricated the "Doctors' Plot," accusing predominantly Jewish physicians of conspiring to assassinate Soviet leaders
examples: This leader accused "saboteurs" of trying to overthrow him in a fabricated scheme he blamed on Jewish physicians, known as the Doctor's Plot.
This leader led the anti-semitic "Doctor's Plot" campaign in which he accused several Jewish physicians of plotting to assassinate leaders of his government.

clues.clue: Attacked "rootless cosmopolitans" in a broader antisemitic campaign, labeling many Jews "cosmopolitan" while dictating the Great Patriotic War's narrative
examples: This man attacked "rootless cosmopolitans" in an antisemitic campaign.
Following World War II, Stalin labeled a number of these people as "cosmopolitan" in his attempt to dictate the narrative of the "Great Patriotic War."

clues.clue: Forced Russian Jews toward the Jewish Autonomous Oblast around Birobidzhan in response to pogroms
examples: This Soviet premier forced Russian Jews to move to the Jewish Autonomous Oblast in response to pogroms perpetrated around the country.
Name this leader, during whose reign Birobidzhan was founded as the center of the Jewish Autonomous Oblast.

clues.clue: Ordered the death of theater director Solomon Mikhoels
examples: This leader, who ordered the death of theater director Solomon Mikhoels, later implicated medical professionals in the Doctors' Plot.

works.name: Foreign Communist Relations
works.description: Stalin's split with Yugoslav leader Josip Broz Tito opened the Informbiro period of intra-Communist conflict, while he also purged the Romanian Communist Ana Pauker, orchestrated a 1929 coup in Tannu Tuva, and hosted foreign visitors from Jawaharlal Nehru's India to Zhu De's China.

clues.clue: Split with Yugoslav leader Josip Broz Tito, opening the Informbiro period and expelling Yugoslavia's Communist Party from Cominform via a 1948 resolution
examples: name this cooling of relations between the leaders of Yugoslavia and the Soviet Union.
The Informbiro period was exacerbated by Stalin's split with this leader of the Partisans who was president of Yugoslavia until his death in 1980.

clues.clue: Purged Romanian Communist Ana Pauker after she met with him
examples: Pauker was purged by Gheorghe Gheorghiu-Dej after a meeting with this Soviet leader.

clues.clue: Orchestrated a 1929 coup in Tannu Tuva over its leader Donduk Kuular's association with Buddhist monks, and like Tuva's government, opposed Lenin's New Economic Policy
examples: This leader orchestrated a 1929 coup in Tuva due to Donduk Kuular's association with Buddhist monks.

clues.clue: Met with Jawaharlal Nehru, who could not stop discussing actor Prithviraj Kapoor's performance in the film Awaara
examples: When Nehru met with this world leader, he wouldn't stop talking about Kapoor's performance in Awaara.

clues.clue: Chinese People's Liberation Army founder Zhu De gave a speech defending him
examples: One demand of the Georgian revolt was to meet with this visiting Chinese dignitary. This founder of the People's Liberation Army had given a speech defending Stalin.

works.name: Cult of Personality: Depictions in Visual Art
works.description: Stalin was a constant subject of Soviet propaganda painting and statuary — Isaak Brodsky and Boris Vladimirsky painted him at his desk, Otakar Svec built the largest depiction of him in Prague, and countless other statues (many later relocated to "fallen monument" parks) stood across the USSR — as well as of foreign satire depicting him as a monster, and of film, including the fifteen movies in which he was played by Mikheil Gelovani and Armando Iannucci's satirical The Death of Stalin.

clues.clue: Depicted as an eight-legged monster by the magazine Simplicissimus and the Catholic Library Service; many of his statues were later relocated to "fallen monument" parks like Lithuania's Grutas Park
examples: Simplicissimus and the Catholic Library Service both depicted this man as an eight-legged monster, and other depictions of him were moved to Grutas Park.

clues.clue: Painted standing behind a desk (twice) by Isaak Brodsky, and standing and gesturing with a red pencil by Boris Vladimirsky
examples: Isaak Brodsky twice showed him standing behind a desk, and Boris Vladimirsky showed him standing and gesturing with a red pencil.

clues.clue: The largest depiction of him, a massive Prague statue by Otakar Svec (who often showed him smoking a pipe), was destroyed in 1962
examples: Often shown smoking a pipe, Otakar Svec created the largest depiction of this man.
That massive statue in Prague was destroyed in 1962, and many other statues of him can now be found in Fallen Monument Park.

clues.clue: Fifteen films portrayed him via actor Mikheil Gelovani, whose perfectly accurate Georgian accent Stalin disliked despite otherwise liking the films
examples: This person liked fifteen films in which he was portrayed by Mikheil Gelovani, but greatly disliked Gelovani's perfect reproduction of his Georgian accent.

clues.clue: Fictionalized in Armando Iannucci's satirical film The Death of Stalin, in which actors like Steve Buscemi used their own accents rather than Russian ones
examples: actors like Steve Buscemi use their own accents in an Armando Iannucci satire of what politician's death?

works.name: Literary Depictions & Denunciations
works.description: Stalin haunts Soviet-era literature above all through Osip Mandelstam's fatal 1933 "Stalin Epigram" and Anna Akhmatova's Requiem, but he also appears as Koestler's "No. 1," a caller in Grossman's Life and Fate, the subject of Solzhenitsyn's gulag writing, and a target of poets and novelists from Yevtushenko and Kadare to Neruda and Szymborska.

clues.clue: The target of Osip Mandelstam's 1933 "Stalin Epigram," which describes his fingers as "ten thick worms" and "huge laughing cockroaches" on his top lip, leading to Mandelstam's arrest and exile
examples: In a poem, this person is described as "ringed with the scum of chicken-necked bosses" and having "huge laughing cockroaches on his top lip."
His thick fingers are described as bulky and fat like live-baits in a poem titled after this man's epigram.

clues.clue: Fictionalized as "No. 1" in Arthur Koestler's novel Darkness at Noon, in which the character Rubashov is executed by his regime
examples: name this character who is referred to as No. 1 in Darkness at Noon.
This subject of an "epigram" by Osip Mandelstam was fictionalized as Number One in Arthur Koestler's novel Darkness at Noon.

clues.clue: Appears as a character in Vasily Grossman's novel Life and Fate, personally calling the physicist Viktor Strum to reinstate him after his ostracism
examples: After being ostracised for refusing to renounce his work, physicist Viktor Strum returns to his job after this man calls him in Vasily Grossman's Life and Fate.

clues.clue: His rule and the fate of a typical "zek" (prisoner) are documented in Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn's The Gulag Archipelago
examples: An autobiographical novel in three volumes set during this leader's rule describes the timeline of a typical "zek." Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn's The Gulag Archipelago describes the era of rule of what Soviet dictator?

clues.clue: Anna Akhmatova's long poem Requiem, circulated via samizdat, memorializes the victims of his purges, including her own son Lev's arrest
examples: Anna Akhmatova's Requiem memorializes the victims of the purges of this Soviet leader.
Requiem is a response to the terror brought about by this ruler of Russia, who started a campaign against Akhmatova after World War Two.

clues.clue: His time in power is depicted in Anatoli Rybakov's novel Children of the Arbat
examples: This man's time in power is treated in the novel Children of the Arbat.

clues.clue: Wrote the pamphlet "Dialectical and Historical Materialism"
examples: name this author of the pamphlet "Dialectical and Historical Materialism" who purged folks like Nikolai Bukharin.

clues.clue: Satirized in Soviet-era fiction: as a urinal statue ridden by statesmen in The Yawning Heights, as the addressee of a prefatory letter in the story collection The Dragon, and removing his mustache "like a prop" in the story "The Circle of Friends"
examples: In a literary parody of this person, the painter Artis draws a perfect likeness of this man's penis being ridden by a few world statesmen on a urinal erected in his honor - that novel is The Yawning Heights.

clues.clue: Yevgeny Yevtushenko's poems "The Heirs of Stalin" and "Babi Yar" (set to music by Shostakovich) are read as attacks on his lingering influence and antisemitism
examples: Yevtushenko wrote a 1961 poem titled for the "heirs of" this deceased Soviet leader.
Yevtushenko's "Babi Yar" is often perceived as an attack on the anti-semitism of this Soviet premier.

clues.clue: Ismail Kadare's novel A Dictator Calls centers on him "rolling executions on his tongue like berries," referencing the Mandelstam affair
examples: The title of Kadare's A Dictator Calls refers to this man, who rolls "executions on his tongue like berries" in the "Epigram" that got Mandelstam arrested.

clues.clue: Praised in Pablo Neruda's poem collection Canto General
examples: name this ideology advanced by Canto General's paean to Stalin.

clues.clue: Implicitly compared to a yeti in Wislawa Szymborska's poem "Notes from a Nonexistent Himalayan Expedition"
examples: In a poem by a non-Russian author, Stalin is implicitly compared to this creature, who is told "we've got Shakespeare there [...] we play solitaire and violin."

works.name: Death, Denunciation & Legacy
works.description: Stalin's death in 1953 led to his denunciation by successor Nikita Khrushchev in the 1956 "Secret Speech," which opened the censorship-easing "Khrushchev Thaw" and touched off both the Hungarian Revolution (where protesters beheaded his statue) and a Georgian revolt demanding his memory be honored; historians have since compared his regime to Nazi Germany's.

clues.clue: Denounced by his successor Nikita Khrushchev in the 1956 "Secret Speech," which noted "not even the czars created prizes they named after themselves"
examples: name this dictator whose reputation was harmed by the denunciation of his successor, Nikita Khrushchev.
A speech denouncing this leader noted that "not even the czars created prizes they named after themselves."

clues.clue: His death and denunciation opened the censorship-easing "Khrushchev Thaw," during which samizdat literature (including Akhmatova's poems) began to circulate and places like Mount Stalin and Stalingrad were renamed
examples: In the "thaw" after this leader's death, underground samizdat literature began to circulate, including poems by Anna Akhmatova.
The 1956 revolt was a protest of this initiative, which included relaxations on censorship and the renaming of places such as Mount Stalin and Stalingrad.

clues.clue: Protesters beheaded a statue of him in one of the iconic images of the 1956 Hungarian Revolution, sparked partly by Khrushchev's denunciation
examples: In one of the iconic images of the Revolution, protesters beheaded a statue of this leader.

clues.clue: A 1956 revolt in his native Georgia demanded that his memory be honored
examples: by 1956, the March 9 Massacre here targeted loyalists who demanded that Stalin's memory be honored.

clues.clue: Historians have compared his regime to Nazi Germany's, notably in Hannah Arendt's The Origins of Totalitarianism and Timothy Snyder's Bloodlands
examples: identify these two systems, which were studied by Hannah Arendt in The Origins of Totalitarianism and which clashed during World War II.
Stalin's post-War anti-Semitic campaign is discussed in this book by Timothy Snyder.

works.name: Family: Svetlana Alliluyeva
works.description: Stalin's daughter, Svetlana Alliluyeva, later defected to the United States after a trip through India, Rome, and Geneva, and went on to marry the son-in-law of architect Frank Lloyd Wright.

clues.clue: His daughter Svetlana Alliluyeva defected to the United States, later writing the memoir Twenty Letters to a Friend
examples: Name this daughter of Joseph Stalin, who defected to the United States.

clues.clue: Before defecting, Svetlana traveled to Sochi with her lover Kumwar Brajesh Singh; she later married the son-in-law and architect disciple of Frank Lloyd Wright
examples: Svetlana later married the son-in-law and architect disciple of this founder of the Prairie School.

comprehensive_summary: Joseph Stalin (Ioseb Besarionis dze Jughashvili) was born and raised in Georgia, where as a youth he wrote Georgian-language poetry (including "To the Moon") under the pen name "Soselo" and memorized Shota Rustaveli's national epic The Knight in the Panther's Skin. Historians dispute whether he personally participated in or merely watched the Tiflis bank robbery he had planned, and he agitated as a radical in the oil city of Baku — where he said his "last warm feelings for humanity" died when his wife perished from the city's pollution — before gaining military fame at Tsaritsyn. Rising through the party under aliases like "Koba," he worked with Nikolai Bukharin to adopt "Socialism in One Country" over Lenin's New Economic Policy, and succeeded Lenin as leader of the Soviet Union. His forced collectivization and dekulakization produced the Holodomor, a catastrophic Ukrainian famine exacerbated by the "Law of Spikelets" and denied by journalists like Walter Duranty (against Gareth Jones's reporting); he wrote the article "Dizzy with Success" to blame "excesses" for the famine, had census-takers of TsUNKhu arrested after a census showed a population decrease, and deported kulaks to labor camps before reversing course after Operation Barbarossa. His kolkhoz system, built through Five-Year Plans, also produced a famine that killed a fourth of Kazakhstan's population, while in Central Asia he launched the anti-veiling Hujum campaign ("To the Attack!"), arrested reformist Jadidi writers like Abdurauf Fitrat, forcibly deported Koreans and Tatars by cattle car, and executed the Uzbek SSR's leadership; he also closed churches under his Second Five-Year Plan before reversing course during the Great Patriotic War. Sparked by the assassination of Sergei Kirov, his Great Purge (Great Terror) of the late 1930s used show trials — the Trial of the Sixteen, the Shakhty and Metropolitan-Vickers "wrecking" trials — and NKVD chiefs Nikolai Yezhov and then Lavrentiy Beria to eliminate rivals, purging his former ally Nikolai Bukharin, executing military theorist Mikhail Tukhachevsky and the Red Army's generals, and sending dissidents like Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn to the gulag; Katyusha rocket designer Georgy Langemak was executed in the purge, and photographs were doctored to erase enemies like Lev Kamenev and Grigory Zinoviev. After signing the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact with Nazi Germany to split Poland, Stalin's purge-weakened Red Army was invaded in Operation Barbarossa; his NKVD carried out the Katyn Forest Massacre of Polish officers (later blamed on the Nazis), and he relied on general Georgy Zhukov — hero of Khalkhin Gol and the occupation of Bessarabia — through the pivotal Battle of Stalingrad, where Zhukov's Operation Uranus encircled and forced the surrender of Field Marshal Friedrich Paulus's 6th Army after German troops trapped in "the Cauldron" ate their own horses; Erich von Manstein's relief attempt, Operation Winter Storm, failed and was followed by the final Soviet assault, Operation Ring. Stalin met Churchill and Roosevelt as one of the "Big Three" at the Yalta and Tehran Conferences, saw Operation Bagration ranked as the "fifth of the ten blows" of the war, and picked Vyacheslav Molotov to lead the Soviet atomic bomb project. In his final years he fabricated the antisemitic "Doctors' Plot," accused Jews of "rootless cosmopolitanism," ordered the death of theater director Solomon Mikhoels, and forced Jews toward the Jewish Autonomous Oblast around Birobidzhan. Abroad, his split with Yugoslav leader Josip Broz Tito opened the Informbiro period and got Yugoslavia's Communist Party expelled from Cominform; he also purged Romanian Communist Ana Pauker, orchestrated a 1929 coup in Tannu Tuva, met Jawaharlal Nehru (who discussed actor Prithviraj Kapoor's film Awaara), and was defended in a speech by China's Zhu De. Stalin was a constant subject of propaganda art — painted at his desk by Isaak Brodsky and Boris Vladimirsky, memorialized in Otakar Svec's since-destroyed Prague statue, and satirized abroad as an eight-legged monster — as well as of film, played by Mikheil Gelovani in fifteen movies (whose accurate Georgian accent Stalin disliked) and later satirized in Armando Iannucci's The Death of Stalin. In literature, he is the target of Osip Mandelstam's fatal 1933 "Stalin Epigram" and Anna Akhmatova's Requiem, appears as "No. 1" in Arthur Koestler's Darkness at Noon and as a caller in Vasily Grossman's Life and Fate, is documented in Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn's The Gulag Archipelago and Anatoli Rybakov's Children of the Arbat, wrote the pamphlet "Dialectical and Historical Materialism," and was satirized in The Yawning Heights, The Dragon, and "The Circle of Friends," while inspiring Yevgeny Yevtushenko's poems, Ismail Kadare's A Dictator Calls, Pablo Neruda's praise in Canto General, and an implicit comparison to a yeti in Wislawa Szymborska's poetry. After his 1953 death, successor Nikita Khrushchev denounced him in the 1956 "Secret Speech," opening the "Khrushchev Thaw" of relaxed censorship and touching off both the Hungarian Revolution (where protesters beheaded his statue) and a Georgian revolt demanding his memory be honored; historians including Hannah Arendt and Timothy Snyder have since studied his regime alongside Nazi Germany's. His daughter, Svetlana Alliluyeva, later defected to the United States and married the son-in-law of architect Frank Lloyd Wright.
